WRITEUP
CAPESIZE
The market ended the week on a softer note, with the BCI 5TC slipping by $428 to settle at $30,052. In the Pacific, activity was limited, with only one miner in the market and sentiment easing as reports emerged of $10.00 being paid on C5, pressuring the index lower. As a result, the C5 index fell by 0.405 to close the week at $10.095. In contrast, the South Brazil and West Africa to China trade has become increasingly date sensitive. Brokers reported a notable tightening in ballasting tonnage, lending underlying support to the route, with market talk centring on mid to upper $24s being paid on C3. Reflecting this firmer undertone, the C3 index edged up by 0.104 to finish the week at $24.177.
Atlantic
Trafigura is reported to have fixed the Jijiao controlled Lady Dawn (211,041 2022) for 190,000/10 from Sudeste to Qingdao with 23 January cancelling at $24.00. Kline won the Ore and Metal tender yesterday for 170,000/10 from Saldanha Bay to Qingdao for 19/24 January at $16.15 and CMB won todays tender for 170,000/10 from Saldanha Bay to Dangjin for 21/25 January at $16.90.
Asia
Rio Tinto fixed a TBN for 170,000/10 from Dampier to Qingdao for 5/7 January at $10.00, lacking further details.
PANAMAX
A quiet Friday but still with a clear downward trend, with sentiment steadily weakening amid a persistent lack of fresh fixture activity across both the Atlantic and Pacific basins. Trans Atlantic and fronthaul rates came under continued pressure as charterers pushed for lower levels and owner resistance progressively softened. In Asia, conditions deteriorated further, with a scarcity of cargoes and limited replenishment keeping activity subdued. Although a handful of Indonesian tenders surfaced, interest was largely restricted to smaller and older Panamax units. An oversupply of prompt tonnage further undermined market fundamentals, allowing charterers to circulate increasingly aggressive rate ideas for early cover. Period activity remained muted throughout, and the P5TC average fell by $597 to close at $11,908.
Atlantic
The Indian Harmony (75,385 2013) open Gibraltar 21/22 December fixed for a trip via US Gulf redelivery China at $18,000 with Refined Success. Further south, the Yangze 26 (82,429 2024) Kandla 18/20 December fixed for a trip via EC South America redelivery Singapore-Japan at $14,000 to Messrs Bunge. An unreported charterer was said to have taken the Minoan Marathon (81,945 2015) basis EC South America 5/6 January for a trip to the Far East at $15,250 + $525,000bb but others suggest the rate was $16,500 + $650,000bb.
Asia
The Anny Petrakis (75,181 2008) Taiwan 18 December fixed a trip via Indonesia redelivery South China at $5,250 but a charterer was not reported.
SUPRAMAX
With the upcoming holidays next week, it was a rather dull affair today, with many away from their desks to celebrate the festive season. The downward momentum recently seen from the US Gulf remained in play with little in the way of fresh enquiry, although some felt a bottom may have been found. Another day of little resistance from the South Atlantic again more enquiry would be needed to stop the downward motion. The Continent-Mediterranean remained finely balanced but little fresh fixing information surfaced. From Asia, little sign of any change in direction as charterers remained firmly in the driving seat and happy to wait. The 11TC finished the week down a further $463 to close at $15,441.
Atlantic
The Ionic Smyrni (56,025 2013) open Itaqui 29/30 December was linked to Contilines covering a vessel basis Praia Mole with steels at close to $20,000 redelivery US Gulf.
Asia
The Syros Trader (53,408 2008) Yangpu 21/23 December was heard fixed for a trip via Indonesia redelivery China at $7,200, but no more details came to light.
HANDYSIZE
A typically slow Friday for the Handy sector, with limited visible activity. The BHSI closed at 746, while the 7TC average settled at $13,432, down by $209. Activity appears to have eased in the Continent and Mediterranean, with overall sentiment remaining positional; fixtures emerging indicate levels below the last done. In the South Atlantic and US Gulf, sources are reporting fewer fresh enquiries entering the market, with bid–offer levels significantly lower than yesterday. The Pacific market continues to maintain a quiet tone, with tonnage continuing to build and rates trending broadly softer across the region.
All the above is given in good faith but wog. E&OE.
Thanks and best regards.
